sentences.text: Witness, too.
Date of the interview, you've got already.
My name is Henrietta Altman.
People call me by my pet name, actually.
It's not a given name, nothing formal about it.
And it's Kicia.
Henrietta-- H-E-N-R-I-E-T-T-A.
And Kitia-- K-I-T-I-A.
And Altman-- A-L-T-M-A-N.
Didn't do so much spelling in my life, but--
My name at-- maiden name was Szpigelman .
The Polish version of Henrietta was my given name at birth-- Henrika.
I was born in Poland.
The 1st of March, 1922.
Well, work it out.
I can't make it so easy for the viewers.
I was born in Poland in a little town by the name of Bedzin.
Do you want me to--
--spell that?
B-E-D-Z-I-N.
And it was in the eastern-upper Silesia.
That was the part of Poland where it was located.
I come from a very close lineage, and very loving-- I suppose everybody says the same-- family.
I had one brother only.
My father's name was Moishe, which equals Moses.
And my mother's name was Zofia, Sophie.
My brother's name was Aaron.
We lived in there with my mother's brother and sisters in one huge, family home in one of the main streets in town.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And the rest of the family lived nearby, not-- except of one sister, my mother's sister, who lived in Palestine.
My mother's whole family lived in Bedzin.
However, my father's family lived-- five brothers of his-- lived since the World War I in France, in Paris.
And I met them only after the war, with the exception of one.
And two sisters lived-- three sisters lived in different towns than Bedzin.
So my mother's family was much more concentrated, and therefore we were much more interacting with my mother's family.
Another characteristic thing about my mother's family was-- which was unusual for Jewish, Jewish any class family-- that the daughters had higher educations, but not the sons.
I couldn't tell you.
It was all acquired before I was born.
I think it was due, mainly, to my grandfather who was a very broad-minded man, and he was actually one of the founder of the first Jewish boys' high school, Yavne.
Yavne.
Yes.
My grandfather's name was-- wait a second-- Hanoch, because I had been named after him, Erlich.
Erlich.
I think there was another name, but I forgot-- Shia-- Shia Hanoch, or Hanoch Shia-- something like that-- Erlich.
Oh, God-- Makes you realize how many things you forget.
And he died very young, considerably young.
And the legend in our times, in our family, went that--
Yes, the legend in our family went that he was a very heavy smoker.
He came from a long chain of tobacco merchants, and his own father was known-- out part of Poland was belonging, at times, to Russia, at times to Austro-Hungarian monarchy.
But that was in times of Russia.
And the fame of his skill of mixing various tobaccos were as far as Moscow, and he was getting orders from the officers to send them tobacco.
And I still remember a stair leading from my grandmother's kitchen with a door, and that was leading to the shop.
And he was to go at night, when everybody was asleep, and prepare his mixtures.
And they ask him, when will you give us the recipe?
He said, before I die.
And lo and behold, he dropped dead without giving anybody the-- [LAUGHS] the recipe for the--
So my grandfather, actually, very soon abandoned the tobacco business.
But one habit remained was he was the heavy smoking, and what I think now is that he contracted lung cancer.
And the doctor advised him to go to Berlin and undergo an operation.
And the year was before my birth.
It must have been '21, 1921.
And he went to a rabbi, as it was the wont of Jews at that time, and the rabbi told him to go and to have the operation, and he died soon after the operation.
He never came back to--
In Berlin.
Oh, they brought his body back.
And his sons, in bitterness and resentment, went to see the rabbi, and they said, how could you advise my father-- how could you advise my father, go to Berlin and have the operation?
Look what happened.
He said, he was a too good man to stuffer.
So he knew.
He knew, and he said, he was a too good man to suffer.
So they brought the body back.
The family was devastated.
And I was the first-born grandchild after his death.
And as his name was Hanoch.
I was named-- Hanoch, which means, more or less, in Polish Henry-- and I was named Henrika.
And that's the story of my name.
Oh, there is another beautiful story that my mother actually intended to call me, if I am a girl, Michelle, and if a boy, Michal.
And as she was in labor with me, and going to hospital was only for poor people, so she was in labor in our own place, surrounded by family, mothers, and sisters.
And in between the labor pain, she fell asleep.
She dozed off, and she dreamt that her father came to see her.
She was his favorite--
Daughter.
--everybody says she was his favorite daughter.
And he leaned on the foot-- on the foot of her bed and said, Zofia, my darling, don't worry.
Everything here will be all right.
And when she woke up, she said, if I have a boy, it will be Henry, and if I will have a girl, it will be Henrietta.
Yes?
Well, we didn't live in another family.
Every family had his-- his or her own flat on a different floor.
It was a huge house, three stories, and U-shaped apartment house, not unlike this one.
And in front were living-- we lived on the first floor.
My other aunt lived on the second floor.
My uncle lived on the first floor next to us.
My grandmother lived also on the first floor.
So we were all-- in a way, it was all like one, big family, but each one had it's own apartment.
Their own privacy.
A large apartment.
Well--
Or--
My--
No.
Courtyard were not for children from very good-- not for girls.
I think I had a governess till I was about five or six.
My brother used to play with boys, but I don't remember ever playing with other children because I was rather a sickly child.
And I think I started even school a year later because I was sickly-ish.
And-- But I definitely-- my-- I was the youngest in the family till I was 13, so I didn't play with-- they played with me, my oldest cousin.

And I basked in their glory, and I forever rummaged in their drawers for love letters, in their wardrobes to dress up, tried on their cosmetics, and generally I made myself a nuisance, but everything was forgiven.
I was the youngest in the family for 13 years.
When I was 13, the youngest sister of my mother, who was a dental surgeon, had a girl.
And-- and that was in '35-- no, '22?
In '35-- Marusha was born in '35, and I absolutely adored my aunt.
What was your aunt's name?
My aunt's name was Rachel, but we called her Rue.
And I don't think that I ever, ever in my life loved anybody with so much intensity, with so much devotion, with so much abandon as I loved her.
Well, maybe because she wasn't married.
She-- she was married, actually, for one year to a man who had tuberculosis, and she was not aware of it.
That's another story that I heard in my family repeated and repeated-- over the marriage was barely consummated, and he got an attack of tuberculosis.
And for the rest of his life, he was a figure in Vienna sanatorium.
And I was getting beautiful clothes every time his wife went to visit him.
My mother or my father went to visit him.
And I still remember some crocheted bonnets in multicolored circles around the ears, some dresses.
I don't remember ever meeting him, and my aunt was for six or seven years a widow.
And then she married a man who was-- his name was Samuel Cygler, and he was a painter.
And she had many, many men who wanted to marry--
Artist.
[LAUGHS] Well, in Polish, you have got different words.
How do you call a painter, an artist painter, in English?
An artist could be anything.
Oh, I see.
Well, I don't know.
I don't know.
Well, he was an artist.
He was a painter, and I liked him most of all.
And eventually, of course, it wasn't my choice that decided.
She married him.
And in 1935, they had a child.
In those times, they say that you shouldn't have children after 30, that the time for childbearing finish was 25 or 26.
Yes.
And the child that was born was a very beautiful girl.
Of course it was all right.
I mean, it's only the attitude has changed, but nature hasn't changed very much.
She wasn't helped by nature.
She just naturally had the child, but she was what was already later than usual.
Like marriageable age-- was-- she married-- she must have married at the age of 30-- was considered already late.
I think she was 32.
And she was a little bit concerned that I might feel demoted.
And my mother went with her to a larger town, neighboring town, [NON-ENGLISH], which had the Jewish hospital.
And her being a doctor, had the special conveniences there.
My aunt.
She was a dental surgeon.
And my mother stayed with her for two weeks, non-stop.
And I was allowed to visit.
And on my first visit, my aunt said, this child of mine will be looked after by you when I will be too old to look after her.
So I leave her in your care.
You will take her to her first ball.
You will be listening to her first romantic confessions.
And it was absolutely genuine, but at the same time she left me a legacy to look after that child.
Yes, definitely.
Not only she didn't demote me, but she promoted me.
And she-- in a way, she burdened me with that responsibility.
Well, I will come to it because I actually saved the child's life during the war, and I consider that-- so did everybody, but particular me.
I consider that this is absolutely my--
--my responsibility.
I have to do it.
But the situation-- if you want, I can talk about it now, or if you want--
--I can talk about it in chronological order.
Which way do you want?
Yes.
Well, we were traditional.
We-- I mean father, mother, and so on-- were traditional.
Not particularly observant as far as the lesser holy days.
This is not a description according to Judaism.
To Judaism there are no lesser holidays, all are equally important.
And my mother kept kosher, but I think it was part of a culture, rather than of a belief.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My parents went to synagogue on Rosh Hashanah and on Yom Kippur.
Well, I did it for fun like all teenagers did.
I wasn't forced to do it.
I went to a Jewish school.
It was a private Jewish school which had a very long name because it had the name of the founder, and the founder were [NON-ENGLISH].
So it was a Jewish gymnasium in the name of family [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a private school that had all the right of-- here you say-- we said state school.
And here, state school is not a private school, but in Poland, to have the right of state school, means to have the highest rights as any highest educational institution has got.
Yes.
It was very elitist and very selective.
But like most of Jewish institutions, there was always room for charity, and the families who could afford to pay paid in such a way that in fact you paid for another child.
And I learned Hebrew from the time zero, from the--
Not davening.
Yes.
We started with Hebrew from grade one, I think, or grade two.
I even remember that Bialik came to visit our school, and I presented him with a bouquet of flowers and recited one of his poems.
Yes.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
--translation is that, on the window stood a little dove.
A boy came, and the dove flew away.
And the boy cried.
And the boy cried.
So that's the rough translation of it.
I suppose that he gave me even-- planted a kiss on my forehead.
Even so.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't-- I don't remember that anything was imposed, as far as religion is concerned, in our house, except perhaps to use ham on the plate.
But we were allowed to buy ham, particularly if someone was convalescing from a sickness, because ham was considered very nourishing-- on glass plates.
Absolutely, but you're not allowed to use the plates.
We could eat the ham either from the paper, or from a glass plate, and that's it.
Yes.
Only for health purposes.
Our maid, I remember, only she could-- she cut the bread roll, and she buttered it.
I don't remember what thing-- that didn't matter anymore whether she used butter, or she used another sort of fat, and she filled that with ham, and we at it.
We never brought non-kosher food to school, but that wasn't a problem because none of the kids there would have brought ham to school.
The-- the situation of synagogues or shuls in Poland was such that they were official synagogues.
And they-- I have got, even somewhere, a picture of the grand synagogue.
And also, the owners of big houses considered it a-- a honor and a responsibility to-- to provide a prayer a house for the tenants.
And my grandfather made such a provision.
And the room was put this aside for that purpose.
It was used for many other purposes.
Among others, [INAUDIBLE] hiding a generation of communists.
The youth in our family, and they were all about eight to 10 years older than I, were very leftist.
And throughout the year it was provided for teaching little children Hebrew and prayers.
And my parents prayed there.
However, when I was older, I used to go with my fathers grandmother, and I remember that it must have been a special occasion, and I went to the grand synagogue.
This grand synagogue was very old.
And my uncle, Samuel Cygler, together with another artist who was a painter, painted the synagogue.
His name was Apfelbaum.
And they painted the frescoes--
--of the synagogue.
It was a very ancient synagogue, still a wooden--
--a wooden structure.
And of course it was burned with 100 people inside-- the first act of German occupation--
--when the war broke out.
They sealed off the people and-- yeah.
Oh no, this room was divided with a sheet.
On one side were women, and on the other side were men.
I think the women were on the side, which throughout the year belonged to a plumber.
I don't think that this particular synagogue was held in a very high esteem by my uncles, and by my father, but as a token of respect, they had to go there to set an example to the others.
The Jewish community in town was sharply divided into the well-to-do and very poor.
Division was-- no.
Actually, I am not right in saying that that was sharp-- a sharp division, because it was absorbed-- the poor people were absorbed within community.
The rest of the community took care of the poor one.
The community was a community with a very high-developed sense-- it was a very high developed sense of volunteerism--
--of charity, and of communal responsibility.
So it's not right that it was hardly-- that was a sharp division.
For instance, I remember we had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was he?
Moshke-- Moshke was, by profession, a porter.
Porters were profession.
Moshke was, by profession, a porter.
And Moshke was very, very poor.
And he never came begging.
And my father never allowed him to come to a situation where he had to beg.
And always, before Friday, he called him to perform a task.
And for that task, he paid him.
He could have well done without the task.
Made sure that the--
--perseverance of his human dignity is unscathed.
And I was brought up in that tradition not knowing any other way.
So what I distinctly remember is that human dignity was sacred.
Absolutely sacred.
If I was rude to our maid.
I was not punished.
I was only given to perform her task for a certain period of time, which in my mind, work is not punishment.
If I was told for two months to set the table because once I sat at the table and I said, there's no fork, or I don't like that spoon, or something like that.
I was told from now on, you will be setting the table.
My brother's best friend was a boy who was disabled, and he studied with my brother.
He was never short of anything, never made to feel that because he's disabled he can't have a uniform, or he can't have another book.
And when they matriculated in '32 and most of the boys pursued their own careers, my own brother went to France to study veterinary science.
My brother sent Leon-- Leon Drexler was his name-- for one year to France, so he, too, will know what it means to be a student.
And he, too, will go overseas.
My brother, no.
My father--
Yes, but not because he was so wealthy.
What I mean--
Yes, but he, I am sure, that he approached some people for contributions because I know that many times people came to ask for contributions.
And it was not an unusual thing to collect money for a dowry.
Yes.
Oh yes.
If there was a poor girl, and the only thing that stopped her from getting married-- I'm sure there were other things that were stopping her, and the-- [LAUGHTER] The dowry was the dangling candy.
They collected for the dowry.
And many times I remember that the wedding took place in my grandmother's apartment.
Complete strangers.
I don't know.
I think it--
--came down-- I think it came down in my genes.
I don't remember that I acquired these things.
I never knew anything else.
I never knew anything else.
There is always this tradition that you have to do things from which others benefit, not for benefit for yourself.
Well, that was part of my culture, of my upbringing.
And as I said, when I think about it, it must have come with my genes, the same way as my hair and my eyes came.
We were very close.
And also, I don't know whether the proximity of Germany was a very contributing factor, but my father was a great admirer of Germans.
He was a Germanophile.
He admired their sense of organization, their sense of order.
He admired their cleanness.
He admired their reliability.
And I remember that my brother, for his bar mitzvah, got the collected works of Goethe and Schiller in gothic.
And we had-- my father had business in connection-- well, interests actually because he had real estate in Berlin.
He made very frequent trips to  Berlin.
And he spoke and wrote German much better than he did Polish.
My father had a normal Jewish education, but not a higher education.
Pardon?
My father was what you would call now a silent partner with his cousin in a coal mine.
That was a coal-mining district, I might as well add.
It was the potbelly of-- of Poland.
The biggest deposits of coal in Poland were in that district.
Well, that was quite sufficient for a very comfortable living.
And also, the fact that we had-- [INAUDIBLE] --real estate in Germany, which provided us with a handsome-- I imagine that must have been quite handsome--
--income.
Very frequently.
From '33 he was not restricted, but his trips were less frequent.
Yes, absolutely.
Well, the type of changes-- he said, [NON-ENGLISH].
For instance, his restaurant where he used to go, tailor-- stop?
